title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
subtitle: 64 y/o female, in the alteplase window
nursesNote: 0712 - 64 y/o female brought in by EMS after sudden onset of right-sided facial droop, right arm weakness, and slurred speech witnessed by spouse at 0635. Last known well 0630. NIHSS at triage: 14 (moderate). Spouse denies recent surgery, head trauma, GI bleed, or anticoagulant use. CT head without contrast at 0745 - no hemorrhage, no early ischemic changes. Neurology at bedside; client is being evaluated for IV alteplase pending BP control.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish this client as a candidate for IV alteplase.

opts.text: Last known well at 0630 (within the 4.5-hour window)
opts.rat: The IV alteplase window is up to 4.5 hours from LAST KNOWN WELL - not from symptom discovery or ED arrival. This single data point is the gatekeeper for thrombolysis (AHA/ASA 2019).

opts.text: Non-contrast CT shows no hemorrhage and no early ischemic changes
opts.rat: A negative head CT excludes intracerebral hemorrhage - the mandatory prerequisite for alteplase. Absence of early ischemic changes does NOT rule out infarct (often invisible <6h).

opts.text: NIHSS 14 (moderate stroke severity)
opts.rat: NIHSS quantifies severity; scores 5-25 derive the greatest benefit from thrombolysis. A 14 supports treatment in an otherwise eligible candidate.

opts.text: BP 204/112 mmHg
opts.rat: Currently EXCLUDES alteplase until lowered to <185/110. This is a barrier to address, not an eligibility cue.

opts.text: Slurred speech (dysarthria)
opts.rat: Confirms stroke but does not specifically establish thrombolytic candidacy - time window, CT, and severity do.

opts.text: Glucose 142 mg/dL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated but rules OUT hypoglycemia mimic. Does not, by itself, establish candidacy.

opts.text: INR 1.0, platelets 245 K/uL
opts.rat: Supportive normal coags - but normal labs are not 'cues' to recognize.

opts.text: NKDA per spouse
opts.rat: Background safety data, not a clinical cue for thrombolysis.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Focal deficits arise from arterial occlusion creating an irreversibly infarcted core surrounded by a salvageable penumbra
opts.rat: The core infarcts within minutes; the penumbra remains viable for hours if reperfusion occurs (Lewis Med-Surg 11e, p. 1395).

opts.text: The elevated BP is largely a compensatory response to maintain cerebral perfusion to the penumbra
opts.rat: Cerebral autoregulation is impaired in the penumbra, making perfusion pressure-dependent.

opts.text: Aggressive BP lowering before reperfusion can extend the infarct by reducing penumbral perfusion
opts.rat: Overshoot lowering converts salvageable penumbra to infarcted tissue (AHA/ASA 2019).

opts.text: The 4.5-hour alteplase window starts from the time of ED arrival
opts.rat: Incorrect - starts from LAST KNOWN WELL.

opts.text: Alteplase reverses damage already completed in the infarct core
opts.rat: Alteplase only salvages the penumbra; the core infarct is irreversible.

opts.text: A negative head CT definitively rules out acute ischemic stroke
opts.rat: CT is sensitive for hemorrhage; acute ischemia is often invisible for 6-24 hours.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute ischemic stroke - large-vessel (likely left MCA) territory
opts.rat: Sudden contralateral focal deficits, NIHSS 14, witnessed onset, and CT excluding bleed make AIS overwhelmingly most likely.

opts.text: Hemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral hemorrhage)
opts.rat: Always in the differential, but the negative non-contrast CT effectively excludes acute hemorrhage now.

opts.text: Stroke mimic - hypoglycemia
opts.rat: Always assessed early because rapidly reversible, but ruled out here by glucose 142.

opts.text: Stroke mimic - complex migraine or Todd's paralysis
opts.rat: Possible but no headache aura history, no witnessed seizure, deficits persistent.

opts.text: Conversion (functional) disorder
opts.rat: Least likely: deficits follow a clear vascular distribution. Diagnosis of exclusion.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for the alteplase pathway in the first hour.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per stroke protocol to lower BP to <185/110 prior to alteplase
opts.rat: Alteplase is contraindicated above this threshold; controlling BP is the immediate gateway to thrombolysis.

opts.text: Administer alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (10% bolus, 90% infusion over 60 min) once eligible
opts.rat: Standard adult dose for AIS within 4.5 hours of last known well; max total 90 mg. Door-to-needle goal <=45 min.

opts.text: Initiate continuous cardiac/BP monitoring with q15-min neuro checks
opts.rat: Mandatory safety baseline for detecting hemorrhagic conversion or angioedema during and after the infusion.

opts.text: Administer aspirin 325 mg PO immediately
opts.rat: Antiplatelets must be HELD for 24 hours after alteplase to avoid hemorrhagic conversion.

opts.text: Bolus 1 L of 0.9% normal saline wide open
opts.rat: No evidence of hypovolemia; would worsen severe HTN.

opts.text: Administer clopidogrel 600 mg loading dose now
opts.rat: Antiplatelet loading is contraindicated peri-thrombolysis - markedly increases ICH risk.

opts.text: Give sublingual nifedipine for rapid BP drop
opts.rat: Causes precipitous, uncontrolled hypotension that can extend the infarct.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Place client on continuous cardiac/BP monitoring and initiate q15-min neuro checks
opts.rat: FIRST - establishes the safety baseline before any intervention.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per protocol to bring BP <185/110
opts.rat: SECOND - alteplase is contraindicated above this threshold.

opts.text: Confirm all alteplase inclusion/exclusion criteria with the neurologist and obtain informed consent
opts.rat: THIRD - final eligibility check immediately before the bolus.

opts.text: Administer alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (10% bolus, then 90% infusion over 60 minutes)
opts.rat: FOURTH - door-to-needle goal <=45 minutes per AHA/ASA.

opts.text: Transfer to the Neuro ICU for ongoing post-thrombolysis monitoring
opts.rat: LAST - ICU-level monitoring is required for at least the first 24 hours.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 24 hours of post-alteplase care, classify each finding as Improving, Worsening, or Unchanged.

opts.text: NIHSS improved from 14 to 6 at 24 hours post-alteplase
opts.rat: A reduction of >=4 points on NIHSS is clinically meaningful and reflects successful reperfusion of the penumbra.

opts.text: BP controlled at 162/88 mmHg throughout the alteplase infusion
opts.rat: Maintaining BP <180/105 during and 24 hours post-alteplase is the AHA/ASA target.

opts.text: Client passes the bedside dysphagia screen and tolerates small sips of water
opts.rat: Permits safe oral intake and PO meds; signals adequate bulbar function.

opts.text: Sudden severe headache, nausea, and vomiting 4 hours after alteplase infusion
opts.rat: Classic presentation of symptomatic ICH post-thrombolysis. STOP infusion if running, stat CT, notify provider.

opts.text: Right-sided facial droop resolved; only mild residual right hand weakness
opts.rat: Resolution of major focal deficits reflects successful reperfusion.

opts.text: Tongue and lip swelling noted 30 minutes into the alteplase infusion
opts.rat: Orolingual angioedema (1-5%, higher on ACE inhibitors). Stop infusion and protect the airway.

opts.text: No bleeding from IV sites, gums, or urinary catheter; no new bruising
opts.rat: Absence of systemic bleeding is positive given alteplase's systemic fibrinolytic effect.

opts.text: NIHSS unchanged at 14 with no new deficits at 24 hours
opts.rat: Stability, not improvement. Lack of NIHSS reduction suggests failed reperfusion - consider thrombectomy evaluation.

title: Acute ST-Elevation MI (STEMI)
subtitle: 58 y/o male, anterior wall infarct, door-to-balloon clock running
nursesNote: 1430 - 58 y/o male presents to ED via private vehicle with sudden onset crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to left jaw and arm, started 45 minutes ago while mowing the lawn. Pain 9/10, diaphoretic, nauseated, anxious. PMH: HTN, hyperlipidemia, 30 pack-year smoker, father died of MI at 56. Currently on lisinopril and atorvastatin (compliance unclear). Initial 12-lead ECG shows 3-mm ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal depression in II, III, aVF. Cath lab activated; cardiologist en route. Patient is alert, oriented x4, denies prior cardiac history.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish this client as having an acute STEMI requiring emergent reperfusion.

opts.text: 3-mm ST elevation in V1-V4 with reciprocal depression in II, III, aVF
opts.rat: ST elevation >=1 mm in two or more contiguous leads is the diagnostic ECG criterion for STEMI. V1-V4 localizes to the anterior wall (LAD distribution). Reciprocal changes confirm a true infarct (AHA/ACC 2022).

opts.text: Troponin I rising from 0.18 to 12.4 ng/mL over 3 hours
opts.rat: A rising troponin pattern with values >99th percentile confirms acute myocardial injury. The trajectory matters more than a single value (4th Universal Definition of MI).

opts.text: Crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to left jaw and arm with diaphoresis
opts.rat: Classic anginal equivalent presentation. Combined with diaphoresis and nausea, this triad is highly specific for acute coronary syndrome in a 58-year-old male with risk factors.

opts.text: BP 162/96 on arrival
opts.rat: Hypertension is a contributing risk factor and a current finding, but it does not by itself ESTABLISH the STEMI diagnosis. The ECG, troponin, and presentation do.

opts.text: Glucose 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated, likely stress hyperglycemia from catecholamine surge. Not a defining cue for STEMI.

opts.text: 30 pack-year smoking history
opts.rat: A risk FACTOR, not a current clinical cue. History informs probability but does not establish acute infarction.

opts.text: Father died of MI at 56
opts.rat: Family history is a risk factor, not a presenting cue.

opts.text: Patient alert and oriented x4
opts.rat: Reassuring (no cardiogenic shock yet) but a normal finding - not a cue OF the infarct.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: ST elevation in V1-V4 indicates transmural ischemia of the anterior LV wall, most likely from acute occlusion of the left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery
opts.rat: V1-V4 are the precordial leads overlying the anterior wall; the LAD supplies this territory. Anterior MIs carry the highest mortality because the LAD perfuses the largest LV mass (Lewis Med-Surg 11e, p. 743).

opts.text: Rising troponin reflects myocyte necrosis as ischemic cardiomyocytes die and release contractile proteins into the bloodstream
opts.rat: Troponin I is released only when myocardial cells die. The rate of rise correlates with infarct size. Peak typically occurs at 12-24 hours.

opts.text: Time is muscle: every minute of LAD occlusion expands the infarct - the door-to-balloon goal is <=90 minutes for primary PCI
opts.rat: Salvageable myocardium decreases exponentially with time. Primary PCI within 90 minutes is the standard of care; if PCI is unavailable within 120 min, fibrinolytics are indicated (AHA/ACC 2022 STEMI guidelines).

opts.text: ST elevation reflects subendocardial ischemia only, not full-thickness damage
opts.rat: Backwards. ST DEPRESSION reflects subendocardial (NSTEMI) ischemia; ST ELEVATION indicates transmural (full-thickness) injury.

opts.text: Troponin elevation always represents acute coronary syndrome
opts.rat: Troponin can also rise in PE, sepsis, myocarditis, renal failure, and severe HF. The clinical context plus ECG plus rising trajectory is what makes the diagnosis.

opts.text: Reciprocal ST depressions in inferior leads suggest a separate inferior infarct
opts.rat: Reciprocal changes are mirror-image findings from the SAME infarct viewed from opposite leads - they CONFIRM the anterior STEMI rather than indicating a second event.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute anterior STEMI from LAD occlusion (Type 1 MI)
opts.rat: Classic presentation, diagnostic ECG, rising troponin, multiple risk factors. Type 1 = atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombus.

opts.text: NSTEMI / Unstable angina
opts.rat: Same disease spectrum but ECG would show ST depression or T-wave inversion, not ST elevation. The 3-mm ST elevation rules this in as STEMI specifically.

opts.text: Acute pericarditis
opts.rat: Can cause chest pain and ST elevation but typically diffuse (not localized to V1-V4), pleuritic, positional, with PR depression. Troponin usually only mildly elevated.

opts.text: Aortic dissection
opts.rat: Tearing chest pain radiating to back; BP differential between arms; widened mediastinum. Important to consider before thrombolytics, but presentation here favors STEMI.

opts.text: Pulmonary embolism
opts.rat: Pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, S1Q3T3 pattern. Troponin can rise but ST elevation in V1-V4 is not the typical pattern.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for this client in the first hour.

opts.text: Activate cath lab and prepare for primary PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact
opts.rat: Primary PCI is the gold-standard reperfusion therapy for STEMI when available within 90 min. Each 30-minute delay increases 1-year mortality by ~7.5% (AHA/ACC 2022).

opts.text: Administer dual antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 325 mg chewed PLUS a P2Y12 inhibitor (e.g., ticagrelor or clopidogrel)
opts.rat: DAPT is a class I recommendation pre-PCI. Aspirin chewed (not swallowed) gives faster absorption. Reduces stent thrombosis and reinfarction.

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac monitoring with defibrillator at bedside
opts.rat: Anterior STEMI carries the highest risk of malignant ventricular dysrhythmias (VF/VT) and cardiogenic shock. Defibrillator must be immediately available.

opts.text: Administer fibrinolytic (alteplase) immediately while awaiting cath lab
opts.rat: Fibrinolytics are reserved for when PCI is NOT available within 120 minutes. With cath lab activated and cardiologist en route, primary PCI is the better choice.

opts.text: Give nifedipine sublingually for BP control
opts.rat: CONTRAINDICATED in ACS - causes reflex tachycardia and increases myocardial oxygen demand, worsening ischemia.

opts.text: Administer IV beta-blocker (metoprolol) within the first hour while BP is 162/96
opts.rat: IV beta-blockers within the first 24 hours INCREASE risk of cardiogenic shock per the COMMIT trial. Oral beta-blockers can be started later if hemodynamically stable. Not a first-hour priority.

opts.text: Administer IV morphine for chest pain refractory to nitroglycerin
opts.rat: Morphine is no longer a first-line MONA component - associated with worse outcomes (CRUSADE registry). Reserved for pain unresponsive to nitrates. Not a first-hour priority.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Apply oxygen if SpO2 <90%, place on continuous cardiac monitor with defibrillator at bedside
opts.rat: FIRST - establishes safety baseline. ABCs and monitoring for the most likely killer (VF) come before any drug. Note: routine O2 in non-hypoxic ACS is no longer recommended.

opts.text: Establish two large-bore IV lines and draw cardiac biomarkers, CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen
opts.rat: SECOND - access is required for medications and contrast; labs guide PCI safety (renal function, coags, platelets).

opts.text: Administer aspirin 325 mg chewed and the prescribed P2Y12 loading dose
opts.rat: THIRD - DAPT is class I and time-sensitive for plaque stabilization and reducing peri-PCI thrombosis.

opts.text: Administer sublingual nitroglycerin q5 min x 3 for chest pain (if SBP >90)
opts.rat: FOURTH - reduces preload and coronary vasospasm. Hold if SBP drops or right ventricular infarct is suspected.

opts.text: Transport to cath lab for primary PCI; provide handoff (SBAR) to interventional team
opts.rat: LAST in this sequence - the goal of all prior steps. Door-to-balloon time clock is running; everything else has been done to make this as safe as possible.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After successful PCI with stent placement to the LAD, classify each post-procedure finding.

opts.text: Chest pain resolved post-PCI; client rates pain 0/10
opts.rat: Resolution of ischemic pain after reperfusion is the most reliable bedside marker of successful PCI.

opts.text: ST elevation in V1-V4 has decreased by >50% on post-PCI ECG
opts.rat: ST resolution >50% within 60-90 min of reperfusion correlates with successful epicardial AND microvascular reperfusion - a positive prognostic marker.

opts.text: Troponin I peaks at 88 ng/mL at 18 hours, then begins downtrending
opts.rat: A predictable peak-and-fall pattern is EXPECTED after reperfusion - troponin keeps rising even after the artery is open as damaged cells finish releasing contents. The downtrend signals stabilization.

opts.text: New onset of frequent PVCs and a 12-beat run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia 30 min post-PCI
opts.rat: Reperfusion arrhythmias can occur but sustained or symptomatic VT is dangerous - notify provider, ensure defibrillator at bedside, check electrolytes (especially K+ and Mg), assess for ongoing ischemia.

opts.text: BP 110/70, HR 78, SpO2 98% on room air, lungs clear bilaterally
opts.rat: Hemodynamic stability with adequate perfusion suggests preserved LV function and absence of cardiogenic shock - the most feared early complication of anterior MI.

opts.text: New holosystolic murmur at the apex with sudden-onset dyspnea and pink frothy sputum 8 hours post-PCI
opts.rat: Acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture or LV remodeling causing flash pulmonary edema - a mechanical complication requiring emergent echo and possible surgery.

opts.text: Femoral access site soft, no hematoma, distal pulses 2+ bilaterally
opts.rat: No bleeding or vascular complication from the PCI access site - an important safety check given antiplatelet load and heparin exposure.

opts.text: Persistent chest pressure 4/10 unchanged from pre-PCI; ECG ST elevation persists
opts.rat: Failed reperfusion (no-reflow phenomenon, failed stent, or wrong vessel). Requires immediate notification of cardiology - may need rescue PCI or CABG. Lack of improvement is itself a critical finding.

title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism
subtitle: 62 y/o female, post-op day 5 hip arthroplasty, sudden dyspnea
nursesNote: 0815 - 62 y/o female POD#5 right total hip arthroplasty, otherwise recovering well, suddenly developed acute shortness of breath while ambulating to the bathroom. Reports sharp right-sided pleuritic chest pain that worsens with inspiration. Anxious, diaphoretic, stating 'I feel like I cannot catch my breath.' Right calf tender and slightly swollen with positive Homans sign noted. PMH: obesity (BMI 34), HTN, oral contraceptive use until 1 year ago. SCDs were ordered but client reports inconsistent use overnight. Lovenox prophylaxis given on schedule. STAT CT pulmonary angiogram ordered; troponin and BNP drawn; cardiology consulted. Client A&Ox4, no prior cardiac history, no leg cast, no recent travel.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish high clinical suspicion for acute pulmonary embolism in this client.

opts.text: Sudden-onset dyspnea with pleuritic chest pain in a post-op patient with unilateral calf tenderness and swelling
opts.rat: Classic PE triad. Post-op immobility is a major Virchow's triad risk factor. Unilateral calf findings strongly suggest DVT as the embolic source (Wells criteria: 3 points for clinical signs of DVT).

opts.text: Progressive hypotension and tachycardia (BP 108/68 -> 88/54, HR 118 -> 132) with hypoxemia despite supplemental oxygen
opts.rat: Hemodynamic instability defines MASSIVE (high-risk) PE - mortality approaches 50% without rapid intervention. RV failure causes obstructive shock. This single finding upgrades treatment to thrombolytics or embolectomy.

opts.text: ABG showing acute respiratory alkalosis with severe hypoxemia (PaO2 58, PaCO2 28, pH 7.48)
opts.rat: The PE-classic ABG: hyperventilation drops CO2 (alkalosis) but the V/Q mismatch from clot prevents oxygen uptake (hypoxemia). PaO2 <60 = respiratory failure threshold.

opts.text: BMI 34 (obesity)
opts.rat: A risk FACTOR for VTE but not a current clinical cue establishing acute PE. History informs probability; current findings establish diagnosis.

opts.text: Prior oral contraceptive use (stopped 1 year ago)
opts.rat: Hormonal therapy is a historical risk factor. Discontinued 1 year ago, so contributes minimally to current event.

opts.text: D-dimer 4,820 ng/mL
opts.rat: Elevated D-dimer is SUPPORTIVE but extremely non-specific - elevated in surgery, infection, malignancy, pregnancy. Useful only when NEGATIVE to rule out PE in low-probability patients. The clinical picture and CTPA establish the diagnosis, not the D-dimer.

opts.text: Lovenox prophylaxis given on schedule
opts.rat: Medication compliance is reassuring history but does not establish or rule out PE - prophylactic doses can fail, especially with inconsistent SCD use.

opts.text: A&Ox4 with no prior cardiac history
opts.rat: Reassuring (no AMS yet) but a normal finding - not a CUE of PE.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: A clot from the deep veins of the leg traveled through the IVC and right heart, lodging in a pulmonary artery and creating a V/Q mismatch (ventilation without perfusion)
opts.rat: Classic embolic pathway. The clot blocks pulmonary artery flow, so alveoli ventilate but cannot exchange gas - explains hypoxemia despite normal lung mechanics (Lewis Med-Surg 11e, p. 561).

opts.text: Acute pulmonary artery obstruction increases right ventricular afterload, causing RV strain and eventually obstructive shock with hypotension and lactic acidosis
opts.rat: The RV is thin-walled and tolerates acute afterload poorly. Elevated troponin and BNP here confirm RV strain. Hypotension and rising lactate signal the transition to massive PE.

opts.text: Hyperventilation lowers PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis), but ventilation cannot correct the hypoxemia caused by the V/Q mismatch
opts.rat: The patient breathes faster trying to compensate, blowing off CO2 - but no amount of ventilation overcomes blocked perfusion. This is why PE patients often need anticoagulation/thrombolysis, not just oxygen.

opts.text: The hypoxemia is best explained by alveolar hypoventilation from chest pain
opts.rat: Splinting from pain reduces tidal volume, but the primary mechanism here is V/Q mismatch from arterial obstruction - hence the very high A-a gradient.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid bolus will improve hemodynamics by increasing RV preload
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Excessive fluid worsens RV distension and septal bowing into the LV, dropping cardiac output further. Modest fluids (~500 mL) only if hypovolemic; vasopressors are preferred for shock.

opts.text: A normal chest X-ray rules out PE
opts.rat: Chest X-ray is often NORMAL or shows nonspecific findings in PE. Definitive diagnosis is by CT pulmonary angiogram (or V/Q scan if CT contraindicated).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with hemodynamic instability
opts.rat: Post-op immobility, unilateral calf signs, sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, classic ABG, troponin/BNP rise, and now hypotension - textbook massive PE.

opts.text: Submassive PE (intermediate-risk) with RV strain
opts.rat: Same disease spectrum without the hypotension. The patient has now progressed past this category as BP dropped to 88/54.

opts.text: Acute MI / STEMI
opts.rat: Troponin is elevated and chest pain is present, but pleuritic quality, hypoxemia, and unilateral calf signs point away from primary cardiac event. Worth considering and ruling out with ECG.

opts.text: Pneumothorax
opts.rat: Sudden dyspnea and pleuritic pain fit, but no trauma history and absent breath sounds would be expected. CXR rules in/out quickly.

opts.text: Acute MI from contrast nephropathy
opts.rat: No contrast given yet (CTPA pending). Distractor.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for this hemodynamically unstable PE.

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask, target SpO2 >=94%
opts.rat: Refractory hypoxemia from V/Q mismatch demands maximal oxygen delivery. ABCs - oxygenation comes first.

opts.text: Initiate IV unfractionated heparin per weight-based protocol (bolus + infusion)
opts.rat: Therapeutic anticoagulation must start immediately when PE suspicion is high - delaying for confirmatory imaging is dangerous. UFH is preferred over LMWH in unstable patients because it is reversible if thrombolysis is given (CHEST guidelines).

opts.text: Prepare for systemic thrombolytic therapy (alteplase) and notify provider that BP is 88/54 with rising lactate
opts.rat: Massive PE with hemodynamic instability is a class I indication for thrombolytics. The 'tPA for stroke is 90 min, tPA for PE is hours' rule applies. Alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours is standard.

opts.text: Administer 2 L of 0.9% NS bolus wide open to raise BP
opts.rat: Excessive fluid worsens RV distension and drops cardiac output. Limit to ~500 mL if hypovolemic; use norepinephrine for shock.

opts.text: Position the client flat (Trendelenburg) to improve venous return
opts.rat: Trendelenburg worsens dyspnea and is contraindicated in respiratory distress. Semi-Fowler's or position of comfort is preferred.

opts.text: Hold all anticoagulation until CTPA confirms PE
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. With high clinical suspicion and instability, anticoagulation is started empirically. Each hour of delay increases mortality.

opts.text: Administer aspirin 325 mg PO and prepare for cardiac catheterization
opts.rat: This is the STEMI pathway. Anchoring on the wrong diagnosis could be fatal here. Aspirin doesn't treat PE.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen, place on continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitoring, sit client up in semi-Fowler's
opts.rat: FIRST - ABCs. Oxygenation, monitoring, and positioning are immediate and free.

opts.text: Establish two large-bore IV lines and draw labs (CBC, BMP, coags, type & screen)
opts.rat: SECOND - access and pre-anticoagulation/pre-thrombolysis labs. Type & screen needed in case bleeding complications develop.

opts.text: Initiate IV heparin per weight-based protocol after confirming no active bleeding contraindication
opts.rat: THIRD - empiric anticoagulation while awaiting CTPA. Stops further clot propagation.

opts.text: Notify provider of hemodynamic deterioration; prepare alteplase and consent for thrombolysis
opts.rat: FOURTH - massive PE requires thrombolytic decision-making. Provider authorizes; nurse prepares the medication and verifies absolute contraindications.

opts.text: Transfer to ICU once stabilized; ongoing q15-min VS, neuro checks, and bleeding precautions
opts.rat: LAST - escalation of care for ongoing monitoring of thrombolysis bleeding risk and RV function.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After thrombolytic administration and ICU transfer, classify each finding.

opts.text: SpO2 improved from 82% to 95% on 4 L NC; RR decreased to 20
opts.rat: Improved gas exchange reflects clot lysis and restored pulmonary perfusion - the primary goal of thrombolysis.

opts.text: BP stabilized at 118/74 with HR 88 off vasopressors
opts.rat: Hemodynamic recovery indicates resolution of obstructive shock and improved RV function. Best clinical marker of successful reperfusion.

opts.text: Repeat lactate 1.4 mmol/L (down from 3.2)
opts.rat: Lactate clearance >=10% within 6 hours indicates restored tissue perfusion and is associated with markedly lower mortality.

opts.text: Sudden severe headache, new-onset confusion, and unilateral pupil dilation 6 hours post-alteplase
opts.rat: Intracranial hemorrhage - the most feared complication of systemic thrombolysis (occurs in ~2-5%). STAT head CT, stop heparin, prepare reversal (cryoprecipitate, FFP), notify neurosurgery.

opts.text: Bleeding from the femoral access site, INR 2.8, hemoglobin dropped from 12.4 to 9.8 g/dL
opts.rat: Major bleeding complication of thrombolysis + anticoagulation. Hold heparin, apply direct pressure, type and crossmatch, prepare for transfusion and possible reversal agents.

opts.text: Right calf swelling and tenderness unchanged but no extension; SCDs in place
opts.rat: The DVT source persists - this is expected and managed with ongoing therapeutic anticoagulation. Stable findings, not improving but not worsening.

opts.text: Troponin downtrending from peak 0.42 to 0.18 ng/mL; BNP also decreasing
opts.rat: Falling cardiac biomarkers reflect resolution of RV strain - the heart is no longer pumping against an obstructed pulmonary circuit.

opts.text: Repeat CTPA shows residual but reduced clot burden in segmental pulmonary arteries
opts.rat: Partial clot resolution is the realistic goal of thrombolysis - not complete clearance. Combined with clinical improvement, this is a positive outcome.

title: Severe Preeclampsia with HELLP Syndrome
subtitle: 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks, sudden RUQ pain and severe HTN
nursesNote: 0530 - 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks gestation admitted overnight from clinic for elevated BP (162/104) and 3+ proteinuria. Reports new-onset severe right upper quadrant pain rated 7/10, persistent frontal headache unrelieved by acetaminophen, and 'spots' in her vision since 0400. Brisk 4+ deep tendon reflexes with 2 beats of clonus bilaterally. Mild facial and pretibial edema noted. Fundus measures appropriate for dates; FHR 142 with moderate variability and no decelerations on continuous EFM. Magnesium sulfate bolus and maintenance ordered; betamethasone 12 mg IM given for fetal lung maturity; OB and MFM at bedside discussing delivery timing. Patient anxious, asking 'Is my baby okay?'

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish HELLP syndrome superimposed on severe preeclampsia.

opts.text: Platelets 82 K/uL with AST 186 and LDH 742
opts.rat: HELLP triad: Hemolysis (LDH >600), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >2x normal), Low Platelets (<100K). All three present here - definitive HELLP diagnosis (ACOG 2020).

opts.text: RUQ pain 7/10 with persistent frontal headache and visual disturbances ('spots')
opts.rat: RUQ pain reflects hepatic capsular stretch from periportal hemorrhage - a hallmark HELLP sign. Headache and visual changes signal CNS involvement and impending eclampsia. These are the 'severe features' that mandate delivery.

opts.text: BP escalating from 168/108 to 182/118 with brisk 4+ DTRs and clonus
opts.rat: Severe-range BP (>=160/110) plus hyperreflexia with clonus indicates CNS irritability and high seizure risk. This is severe preeclampsia by ACOG criteria; combined with HELLP, delivery is indicated regardless of gestational age.

opts.text: FHR 142 with moderate variability and no decelerations
opts.rat: This is a REASSURING finding indicating fetal well-being - not a HELLP cue. Important to monitor but does not establish maternal pathology.

opts.text: Mild pretibial and facial edema
opts.rat: Edema alone is no longer diagnostic for preeclampsia (was removed from criteria years ago). Many normal pregnancies have edema.

opts.text: 32 y/o G2P1 at 34 weeks gestation
opts.rat: Demographic and gestational data - context, not a cue. Does not establish disease.

opts.text: Magnesium level 5.8 mg/dL (therapeutic range)
opts.rat: This is a TREATMENT response, not a disease cue. Therapeutic magnesium is the goal of seizure prophylaxis, not a sign of HELLP.

opts.text: 24-hour urine protein 4.8 g
opts.rat: Confirms the diagnosis of preeclampsia (>0.3 g) but does NOT establish HELLP specifically. HELLP is defined by the H, E, and L laboratory criteria.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Abnormal placentation triggers widespread endothelial dysfunction, causing vasospasm, hypertension, and multi-organ ischemia - delivery of the placenta is the only definitive cure
opts.rat: Preeclampsia is fundamentally a placental disease. Until the placenta is delivered, the cascade continues. This is why delivery is the ultimate treatment regardless of gestational age in severe disease (ACOG).

opts.text: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia from RBCs being sheared as they pass through fibrin-narrowed vessels causes the elevated LDH and dropping hemoglobin
opts.rat: Schistocytes on peripheral smear and rising LDH reflect mechanical RBC destruction - the 'H' in HELLP. This explains the anemia (Hgb 9.8) without obvious bleeding.

opts.text: Hepatic sinusoidal obstruction by fibrin deposits causes periportal necrosis, capsular distension, and the characteristic RUQ pain - subcapsular hematoma rupture is a feared complication
opts.rat: RUQ/epigastric pain in HELLP signals hepatic stretching. Spontaneous liver rupture is rare but catastrophic - any sudden worsening of pain or hemodynamic instability warrants emergent imaging.

opts.text: Magnesium sulfate works as an antihypertensive by blocking calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle
opts.rat: Magnesium is a SEIZURE PROPHYLACTIC, not an antihypertensive. It reduces eclamptic seizures by ~50% but has minimal effect on BP. Labetalol or hydralazine are used for severe HTN.

opts.text: Proteinuria is caused by glomerular damage from hypertension itself, with no relationship to the placental pathology
opts.rat: Backwards. The same endothelial dysfunction that causes HTN also damages the glomerular endothelium - both are downstream of the placental disease.

opts.text: Fetal distress is the primary trigger for delivery in HELLP, not maternal status
opts.rat: MATERNAL deterioration drives delivery in HELLP - the fetus is reassuring here. Delivering for maternal indication even with reassuring fetal status is a classic NCLEX concept.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: HELLP syndrome with severe preeclampsia
opts.rat: All three HELLP criteria present plus severe-range BP, severe features (headache, visual changes, RUQ pain), 4+ DTRs with clonus, and 4.8 g proteinuria. Diagnostic.

opts.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)
opts.rat: Important differential - presents with RUQ pain and elevated transaminases in late pregnancy. Distinguished by hypoglycemia, profound coagulopathy, and elevated ammonia. Less likely here without those features.

opts.text: Acute viral hepatitis
opts.rat: Could explain transaminase elevation but doesn't account for thrombocytopenia, hemolysis, or severe HTN with proteinuria. Always worth ruling out with hepatitis serologies.

opts.text: Cholecystitis or symptomatic cholelithiasis
opts.rat: RUQ pain is consistent but doesn't explain the lab abnormalities or BP findings. Pregnancy increases gallstone risk so worth considering as concurrent process.

opts.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
opts.rat: Pentad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, AKI, fever, neuro changes. Rare and important consideration but the temporal association with pregnancy and proteinuria favor HELLP. ADAMTS13 activity differentiates.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for severe preeclampsia with HELLP at 34 weeks.

opts.text: Continue IV magnesium sulfate at 2 g/hr maintenance and monitor for toxicity (DTRs, RR, urine output, magnesium levels)
opts.rat: Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizure risk by ~50% and is class I in severe preeclampsia. Therapeutic range 4-7 mg/dL. Toxicity signs: loss of DTRs (8-10), respiratory depression (12+), cardiac arrest (>15). Calcium gluconate is the antidote.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol or hydralazine for BP >=160/110 to target 140-150/90-100
opts.rat: Severe-range HTN demands acute treatment to prevent maternal stroke. Goal is gradual reduction (not normotension) to preserve uteroplacental perfusion. ACOG recommends labetalol, hydralazine, or oral nifedipine as first-line.

opts.text: Coordinate with OB team for prompt delivery (induction or cesarean) - delivery is the definitive treatment for HELLP
opts.rat: In severe preeclampsia with HELLP at >=34 weeks, delivery is indicated regardless of cervical favorability. Continued pregnancy worsens maternal disease. Mode of delivery depends on cervical exam, fetal status, and platelet count (CACOG).

opts.text: Administer aspirin 162 mg daily for placental insufficiency
opts.rat: Low-dose aspirin is used for PREVENTION of preeclampsia in high-risk patients starting in early pregnancy - useless and contraindicated once HELLP is established (worsens bleeding risk).

opts.text: Restrict IV fluids to <80 mL/hr and avoid bolus infusions
opts.rat: Wait - this is also INDICATED. Pulmonary edema is a major HELLP/preeclampsia complication. Total intake should generally be 80-125 mL/hr. But the question asks for THREE most time-critical - delivery, magnesium, and BP control take priority over fluid management.

opts.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed RBCs prophylactically
opts.rat: Hgb 9.8 does not require transfusion. Transfuse based on bleeding, hemodynamics, or symptoms - not just lab values. Risk of transfusion reaction outweighs benefit here.

opts.text: Place a Foley catheter and start strict hourly I&O
opts.rat: Useful for monitoring but not as time-critical as magnesium, BP control, and delivery planning. Important for the second tier of care.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Verify magnesium infusion is running correctly, perform DTR/clonus/RR/UOP check, ensure calcium gluconate at bedside
opts.rat: FIRST - the patient is already on mag for seizure prophylaxis. Verifying correct dose and monitoring for toxicity is the highest-priority safety check. Calcium gluconate must be immediately available.

opts.text: Administer IV labetalol per protocol for BP 182/118; recheck BP in 10 minutes
opts.rat: SECOND - severe-range BP with end-organ symptoms (headache, visual changes) requires acute treatment to prevent stroke. Repeat dosing per algorithm if BP remains severe.

opts.text: Place Foley catheter, ensure 2 large-bore IV lines patent, send type and crossmatch for 2 units PRBCs
opts.rat: THIRD - prepare for potential delivery (especially cesarean) and bleeding. Strict I&O monitors for pulmonary edema and renal function.

opts.text: Continuous EFM, position in left lateral tilt, prepare for transfer to L&D suite for delivery
opts.rat: FOURTH - left lateral position improves uteroplacental perfusion. Continuous fetal monitoring is mandatory. Delivery preparation underway.

opts.text: Provide patient/family education on diagnosis, plan, signs of worsening (worsening headache, vision loss, RUQ pain, bleeding), and emotional support
opts.rat: LAST - vital but lower acuity than the immediate medical interventions. The patient has already asked about her baby - addressing her psychosocial needs is part of comprehensive care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After cesarean delivery and ongoing magnesium therapy, classify each postpartum finding.

opts.text: BP gradually decreased from 182/118 to 142/88 by 12 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Gradual BP normalization after delivery is the expected trajectory once the placenta is removed. Most BPs normalize within 6 weeks postpartum.

opts.text: Platelets rose from 82 to 124 K/uL by 48 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Platelet recovery typically begins within 24-48 hours of delivery and reaches normal by 7-10 days. A reliable marker of HELLP resolution.

opts.text: AST and ALT downtrending (AST 186 -> 92, ALT 164 -> 78) at 24 hours postpartum
opts.rat: Falling transaminases indicate hepatic recovery as endothelial dysfunction resolves with placental removal.

opts.text: Sudden severe RUQ pain with hypotension (BP 78/42) and dropping hemoglobin from 9.8 to 6.4 g/dL at 6 hours postpartum
opts.rat: EMERGENCY - subcapsular hepatic hematoma rupture, a feared HELLP complication. STAT abdominal imaging, type and cross 6 units, prepare for emergent surgery, alert ICU and trauma team.

opts.text: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure 12 hours postpartum despite magnesium at 2 g/hr
opts.rat: Eclamptic seizure on therapeutic magnesium - postpartum eclampsia can occur up to 6 weeks out. Protect airway, give IV magnesium bolus, check level (may need higher), rule out other causes (intracranial bleed).

opts.text: DTRs decreased to 1+ with respiratory rate 8/min and serum magnesium 9.4 mg/dL
opts.rat: Magnesium TOXICITY. STOP infusion, give calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly, support ventilation, recheck level. Classic NCLEX concept: loss of DTRs is the EARLIEST sign of toxicity.

opts.text: Patient bonding with newborn in NICU via video, expressing breast milk, asking appropriate questions about discharge
opts.rat: Psychosocial recovery and engagement in newborn care indicate good emotional adaptation. A meaningful outcome beyond the lab values.

opts.text: Urine output 35 mL/hr (was 60 mL/hr); creatinine 1.4 (unchanged)
opts.rat: Borderline urine output and stable creatinine reflect persistent renal vasoconstriction from preeclampsia - not improving but not acutely worsening. Continue strict I&O, avoid nephrotoxins, expect gradual recovery.

title: Pediatric Status Asthmaticus
subtitle: 8 y/o male, severe asthma exacerbation unresponsive to home albuterol
nursesNote: 1845 - 8 y/o male brought to ED by parents for worsening respiratory distress over 6 hours. URI symptoms x3 days. Home albuterol MDI given q2h x 4 doses with minimal relief. Known asthmatic on daily fluticasone (compliance inconsistent per mother). Two prior PICU admissions for asthma. Arrived sitting in tripod position, unable to speak in full sentences ('two...word...phrases'). Audible expiratory wheeze without stethoscope, severe accessory muscle use with intercostal and subcostal retractions, nasal flaring. Initial SpO2 88% on room air. Continuous albuterol nebulizer started at 15 mg/hr; oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg given; ipratropium added. Mother is tearful at bedside. Father en route. Pediatric ICU consulted; respiratory therapy at bedside.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish impending respiratory failure in this pediatric asthmatic.

opts.text: Two-word speech, tripod positioning, severe accessory muscle use with retractions and nasal flaring
opts.rat: These are signs of SEVERE work of breathing. Inability to speak in full sentences is one of the most reliable bedside markers of severe airflow obstruction in children. Tripod and retractions indicate the child is using maximal effort just to ventilate.

opts.text: ABG showing pH 7.32, PaCO2 48 (RISING in an asthma attack)
opts.rat: CRITICAL finding. In asthma exacerbation, patients hyperventilate and PaCO2 should be LOW. A normal or rising CO2 means the child is tiring and CANNOT sustain the work of breathing - impending respiratory failure (Pediatric Advanced Life Support guidelines).

opts.text: Peak flow 32% of personal best after 4 home albuterol doses and continuous neb
opts.rat: Peak flow <40% of predicted/personal best defines severe exacerbation. Failure to respond to bronchodilators after multiple doses indicates status asthmaticus - a medical emergency that may require ICU-level care and possible intubation.

opts.text: Heart rate 148 bpm
opts.rat: Tachycardia is expected from hypoxemia, work of breathing, and beta-agonist therapy. Concerning but not specific - a CUE supporting severity but not the strongest indicator of impending failure.

opts.text: Glucose 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia plus systemic steroid effect. Not specific to respiratory status.

opts.text: URI symptoms x3 days
opts.rat: Common asthma TRIGGER. Historical, not a current cue establishing severity.

opts.text: Mother tearful at bedside
opts.rat: Parental distress is real and important to address - but it's not a clinical cue about the child's respiratory status.

opts.text: Two prior PICU admissions for asthma
opts.rat: Important RISK FACTOR for severe exacerbation and future fatal asthma. History informs probability but does not establish CURRENT severity.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Bronchospasm, airway edema, and mucus plugging cause severe airflow obstruction; air trapping leads to hyperinflation, increased work of breathing, and eventual muscle fatigue
opts.rat: The asthma triad. Acute exacerbations primarily involve bronchospasm; chronic inflammation contributes edema and mucus. As work of breathing increases, the diaphragm fatigues - the gateway to respiratory failure (Lewis Med-Surg/Wong's Pediatric Nursing).

opts.text: Rising PaCO2 in an asthma attack signals respiratory muscle fatigue - the child can no longer move enough air to blow off CO2 despite tachypnea
opts.rat: This is THE most important concept in severe asthma. Early in the attack, hyperventilation drops CO2. A 'normalizing' or rising CO2 is NOT improvement - it's the child running out of energy. Often called 'pseudonormalization.'

opts.text: Hypokalemia is an expected complication of high-dose beta-agonist therapy as albuterol drives potassium intracellularly - increases arrhythmia risk
opts.rat: Continuous albuterol shifts K+ into cells via beta-2 stimulation. K+ 3.2 here reflects therapy, not depletion. Monitor for arrhythmias and consider replacement if symptomatic or further drops.

opts.text: Wheezing intensity directly correlates with severity - the louder the wheeze, the worse the obstruction
opts.rat: DANGEROUS misconception. A SILENT chest in an asthmatic is OMINOUS - it means there's not enough airflow to generate wheezing. Decreasing wheeze can mean improvement OR catastrophic deterioration; clinical context decides.

opts.text: The primary problem in asthma is alveolar destruction, similar to COPD
opts.rat: Asthma is REVERSIBLE airway inflammation and bronchospasm. COPD involves alveolar destruction (emphysema) and is largely irreversible. Critical distinction for treatment and prognosis.

opts.text: Routine sedation should be given to reduce anxiety-driven hyperventilation
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Sedation depresses respiratory drive in a child already at risk for failure. Anxiety often improves with effective bronchodilator therapy and oxygen.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Status asthmaticus with impending respiratory failure
opts.rat: Known asthmatic, classic trigger (URI), failed home/initial therapy, severe work of breathing, peak flow 32%, and rising CO2. Textbook.

opts.text: Severe acute asthma exacerbation responding to therapy
opts.rat: Same disease but progression has moved past simple exacerbation to status asthmaticus given the rising CO2 and inability to speak in sentences.

opts.text: Pneumonia with reactive airway component
opts.rat: URI prodrome and known asthma make this important to rule out with chest X-ray. Could co-exist and explain partial response to bronchodilators.

opts.text: Foreign body aspiration
opts.rat: Always in the differential for acute respiratory distress in children. Less likely with gradual progression over hours and known asthma history, but a CXR helps rule out.

opts.text: Anaphylaxis
opts.rat: Could cause acute bronchospasm but no exposure history, no urticaria, no hypotension, no GI symptoms. Unlikely but worth considering.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for status asthmaticus with impending failure.

opts.text: Continue continuous albuterol nebulizer 15 mg/hr; add ipratropium for synergistic bronchodilation
opts.rat: Continuous beta-agonist is first-line for status asthmaticus. Adding ipratropium (anticholinergic) provides additional bronchodilation through a different mechanism - particularly effective in the first 24 hours of severe exacerbation.

opts.text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate 25-50 mg/kg over 20 minutes
opts.rat: IV magnesium is a smooth-muscle relaxant indicated for severe asthma not responding to first-line therapy. Reduces hospitalization rates in pediatric severe exacerbations (NIH/NHLBI guidelines).

opts.text: Prepare for possible intubation: notify PICU, have airway equipment at bedside, calculate ETT size and weight-based RSI medications
opts.rat: Rising CO2 with exhaustion signals imminent need for mechanical ventilation. Pediatric airway management is challenging - preparation must be ready BEFORE the child crashes. Ketamine is preferred induction agent (preserves bronchodilation and respiratory drive).

opts.text: Administer racemic epinephrine via nebulizer for upper airway swelling
opts.rat: Racemic epi treats croup/upper airway edema, NOT lower airway bronchospasm. Wrong target. Subcutaneous epinephrine is sometimes used for severe asthma but is second-line; standard albuterol works through the same beta-2 receptors.

opts.text: Sedate with IV midazolam to reduce anxiety and decrease oxygen consumption
opts.rat: DANGEROUS pre-intubation - depresses respiratory drive in a child already failing. If sedation is needed for intubation, ketamine is preferred.

opts.text: Initiate cool mist humidified oxygen via face mask at 2 L/min
opts.rat: Cool mist is for croup. The child needs HIGH-flow oxygen at FiO2 sufficient to maintain SpO2 >=92% - typically non-rebreather initially.

opts.text: Administer IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) for presumed bacterial trigger
opts.rat: Most asthma exacerbations are triggered by VIRAL URIs. Antibiotics are reserved for confirmed bacterial co-infection (e.g., pneumonia with infiltrate, fever, purulent sputum) - not the immediate priority.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Position child upright (high Fowler's or tripod), apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather to maintain SpO2 >=92%
opts.rat: FIRST - ABCs. Positioning maximizes diaphragmatic excursion; oxygen addresses hypoxemia. Free, immediate, life-saving.

opts.text: Verify continuous albuterol/ipratropium running, place on continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitoring, ensure suction and BVM at bedside
opts.rat: SECOND - therapy delivery and safety monitoring. Cardiac monitoring critical given hypokalemia and beta-agonist arrhythmia risk.

opts.text: Establish IV access (consider IO if difficult), draw labs, administer IV magnesium sulfate over 20 min
opts.rat: THIRD - escalation therapy when first-line fails. IV access also enables RSI if intubation becomes necessary.

opts.text: Notify PICU and pediatric anesthesia; prepare RSI medications and weight-based ETT for possible intubation
opts.rat: FOURTH - anticipatory preparation. The child showing rising CO2 may need intubation soon; readiness saves minutes when seconds matter.

opts.text: Update parents in clear, simple language; coordinate child life specialist; allow parental presence during procedures when possible
opts.rat: LAST - psychosocial support is essential but follows medical stabilization. Parental presence is associated with reduced child anxiety and better cooperation - integrate when safe to do so.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 2 hours of escalated therapy, classify each finding.

opts.text: Speaking in full sentences, peak flow improved from 32% to 68% of personal best
opts.rat: Speech in sentences is the bedside marker of resolved severe airflow obstruction. Peak flow >60% indicates response to therapy and possible discharge readiness with close follow-up.

opts.text: Repeat ABG: pH 7.40, PaCO2 38, PaO2 88 on 2 L NC
opts.rat: Normalized gas exchange with reduced supplemental oxygen need - successful resolution of the respiratory failure trajectory.

opts.text: Decreased work of breathing, mild scattered wheeze on auscultation, no retractions
opts.rat: Wheeze can become MORE audible as airways open - paradoxically, hearing wheeze where none was heard before can signal improvement. Combined with decreased WOB, this is a positive sign.

opts.text: Silent chest on auscultation, child suddenly calmer with decreased respiratory effort
opts.rat: OMINOUS finding. A silent chest means inadequate airflow to generate wheezing. 'Calm' here often means exhausted and pre-arrest. CALL CODE TEAM, prepare for intubation immediately.

opts.text: Heart rate 168, frequent PVCs noted on monitor, K+ now 2.8
opts.rat: Worsening hypokalemia from continuous beta-agonists with arrhythmia. Hold further K+-shifting therapies if possible, replace potassium IV, continuous monitoring, prepare for arrhythmia management.

opts.text: Fine tremor of hands and mild jitteriness
opts.rat: Expected and benign side effect of beta-agonist therapy. Reassure family, no intervention needed unless tremor becomes severe.

opts.text: Mother and father at bedside calmly stroking child's hair, child resting more comfortably
opts.rat: Family presence and child's comfort reflect both psychosocial recovery and the success of treating the underlying respiratory distress. Anxiety amplifies asthma; comfort speeds recovery.

opts.text: Glucose 178 mg/dL, blood pressure 110/68
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia from steroids/catecholamines is expected and self-limited. BP stable. Neither improving nor worsening - continue monitoring.

title: Acute Upper GI Bleed - Variceal Hemorrhage
subtitle: 54 y/o male with cirrhosis, hematemesis and melena
nursesNote: 0245 - 54 y/o male brought in by EMS for two episodes of large-volume bright red hematemesis at home and multiple black tarry stools over 8 hours. PMH: alcohol use disorder (drinking until 2 weeks ago), Child-Pugh class B cirrhosis with known esophageal varices on prior EGD, ascites, prior episode of hepatic encephalopathy. On lactulose and propranolol at home (compliance unclear). Arrived diaphoretic, pale, tachycardic, with marked abdominal distension and shifting dullness. Patient lethargic but arousable, oriented to person only. Asterixis present. Massive transfusion protocol activated; 2 large-bore IVs placed; type and crossmatch sent; GI fellow at bedside; emergent EGD planned within 12 hours. Ammonia and coag panel pending.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish massive variceal hemorrhage with hemorrhagic shock.

opts.text: Progressive hypotension (92/58 -> 76/46) and tachycardia (128 -> 142) with rising lactate
opts.rat: Class III-IV hemorrhagic shock - hemodynamic decompensation despite compensatory tachycardia. Rising lactate reflects tissue hypoperfusion and anaerobic metabolism. Mortality climbs steeply once SBP drops below 90.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 6.8 with active hematemesis and melena in a known cirrhotic with prior varices
opts.rat: Hgb <7 with active bleeding signals massive blood loss. Variceal hemorrhage carries 15-20% mortality per episode. Prior varices on EGD plus current hematemesis = variceal source until proven otherwise (AASLD guidelines).

opts.text: BUN 58 disproportionate to creatinine 1.6 (BUN:Cr ratio >30)
opts.rat: Disproportionate BUN elevation in GI bleeding reflects digestion of intraluminal blood as a 'protein meal' plus pre-renal azotemia from hypovolemia. The classic GI bleed lab signature.

opts.text: Asterixis and orientation only to person
opts.rat: These reflect HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY (worsened by GI bleed acting as protein load and elevated ammonia), not the hemorrhage itself. An important concurrent issue but not a cue establishing the hemorrhage.

opts.text: Penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis)
opts.rat: Critical for antibiotic selection (avoid beta-lactams) but not a clinical cue of bleeding.

opts.text: INR 2.4 and platelets 62 K/uL
opts.rat: Coagulopathy from cirrhosis CONTRIBUTES to bleeding severity but doesn't establish the hemorrhage - it's a chronic baseline finding in a cirrhotic. The active bleed is established by hematemesis, melena, hypotension, and dropping Hgb.

opts.text: Marked abdominal distension with shifting dullness
opts.rat: Reflects ASCITES from portal hypertension - chronic, not acute. Important context but not a hemorrhage cue.

opts.text: Compliance with propranolol unclear
opts.rat: Non-adherence to non-selective beta-blocker (used for variceal bleeding prophylaxis) is a RISK FACTOR for the bleed - history, not a current cue.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Cirrhosis causes portal hypertension; collateral veins (varices) form to bypass the obstructed liver and rupture under high pressure, particularly at the gastroesophageal junction
opts.rat: The fundamental mechanism. Portal pressure >12 mmHg risks variceal bleeding. Treatment targets reducing portal pressure (octreotide, beta-blockers) and direct hemostasis (band ligation, sclerotherapy).

opts.text: Blood in the GI tract is digested as a protein load, generating ammonia that the failing liver cannot clear - precipitating or worsening hepatic encephalopathy
opts.rat: Explains why this patient's mentation is deteriorating. Lactulose treats both by acidifying the gut (trapping ammonia as ammonium for excretion) and acting as an osmotic laxative to evacuate the blood load. Critical NCLEX concept.

opts.text: Bacterial translocation from the gut during variceal bleeding causes spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) in 20% of cirrhotic GI bleeds - prophylactic IV antibiotics (typically ceftriaxone) reduce mortality
opts.rat: AASLD class I recommendation. SBP prophylaxis with IV antibiotics is mandatory in any cirrhotic GI bleed, even without confirmed infection. Reduces mortality by ~25%. With his PCN allergy, ceftriaxone is contraindicated - use a fluoroquinolone.

opts.text: Aggressive transfusion to a hemoglobin target of >=10 g/dL improves outcomes in variceal bleeding
opts.rat: OPPOSITE. Restrictive transfusion (Hgb threshold 7 g/dL) is preferred in variceal bleeding - over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding risk (Villanueva NEJM 2013). Common NCLEX trap.

opts.text: Vasopressin and octreotide work by directly cauterizing the variceal bleeding site
opts.rat: They reduce portal blood flow through SPLANCHNIC VASOCONSTRICTION - they don't directly stop the bleed. Definitive hemostasis requires endoscopic band ligation or sclerotherapy.

opts.text: Reversal of coagulopathy with FFP and platelets to normalize INR is a priority before endoscopy
opts.rat: Cirrhotic coagulopathy is a re-balanced state, NOT simple anticoagulation. Aggressive FFP can increase portal pressure and worsen bleeding. Targeted product replacement is preferred over normalizing INR.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Acute esophageal variceal hemorrhage with hemorrhagic shock
opts.rat: Known cirrhosis with prior varices, large-volume hematemesis and melena, hemodynamic decompensation. Highest pre-test probability.

opts.text: Bleeding peptic ulcer (PUD)
opts.rat: Most common cause of upper GI bleeding overall - must remain on differential. EGD differentiates and treats both.

opts.text: Mallory-Weiss tear
opts.rat: Forceful vomiting can cause mucosal tears, especially in alcohol use. Less likely with the volume of bleeding here, but possible co-existing finding.

opts.text: Gastric malignancy with bleeding
opts.rat: Cirrhosis increases hepatocellular carcinoma risk; gastric cancers can present with bleeding. Less acute trajectory typically.

opts.text: Lower GI bleed with rapid transit
opts.rat: Hematemesis localizes the bleed proximal to the ligament of Treitz - lower GI source effectively excluded.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for massive variceal hemorrhage with shock.

opts.text: Initiate massive transfusion protocol with balanced ratio (1:1:1 PRBCs, FFP, platelets) targeting Hgb 7-8 g/dL
opts.rat: Restrictive transfusion strategy is gold standard for variceal bleeding (NEJM 2013). Balanced product ratios prevent dilutional coagulopathy. Permissive hemoglobin avoids portal pressure spike that worsens bleeding.

opts.text: Start IV octreotide bolus 50 mcg followed by 50 mcg/hr continuous infusion
opts.rat: Octreotide reduces splanchnic and portal blood flow via somatostatin pathway. AASLD class I recommendation - start as soon as variceal bleeding suspected, before EGD confirms. Continue for 3-5 days post-EGD.

opts.text: Administer IV fluoroquinolone (e.g., ciprofloxacin) for SBP prophylaxis given PCN allergy
opts.rat: Prophylactic antibiotics reduce mortality by ~25% in cirrhotic GI bleeding. With PCN anaphylaxis history, ceftriaxone is contraindicated; fluoroquinolone is the safe alternative.

opts.text: Insert a Sengstaken-Blakemore tamponade balloon as first-line therapy
opts.rat: Balloon tamponade is a TEMPORIZING measure for refractory bleeding only - high complication rate (esophageal rupture, aspiration). Reserved for failure of medical and endoscopic therapy. Not first-line.

opts.text: Administer 2 units FFP to normalize INR before any other intervention
opts.rat: Aggressive FFP can worsen portal pressure and rebleeding. Cirrhotic coagulopathy is rebalanced; correct only with active bleeding using targeted ratios within MTP.

opts.text: Place NG tube to suction for gastric decompression and lavage
opts.rat: NG placement in active variceal bleeding is CONTROVERSIAL and often contraindicated - risk of trauma to the varices, no proven benefit, and the patient's altered mental status increases aspiration risk. Not time-critical.

opts.text: Hold lactulose to prevent diarrhea-related electrolyte losses
opts.rat: OPPOSITE - lactulose should be CONTINUED/INCREASED to evacuate the blood load and reduce ammonia absorption. Critical for managing the encephalopathy.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Position with HOB elevated, suction at bedside, prepare for possible intubation given hematemesis + altered mental status (high aspiration risk)
opts.rat: FIRST - airway is the top priority. Hematemesis plus encephalopathy is a setup for catastrophic aspiration. Many variceal bleed patients require prophylactic intubation before EGD - missed by students focused only on the bleeding.

opts.text: Verify two large-bore IV access; activate massive transfusion protocol; begin balanced product resuscitation
opts.rat: SECOND - circulation. Large-bore access is essential for rapid product delivery. MTP triggers the blood bank to prepare ongoing balanced products.

opts.text: Initiate octreotide infusion and IV fluoroquinolone prophylaxis
opts.rat: THIRD - disease-specific therapy. Both reduce mortality and should start within the first hour of recognition, well before endoscopy.

opts.text: Coordinate emergent EGD with GI; ensure consent obtained from family if patient unable; continuous cardiac monitoring during transport
opts.rat: FOURTH - definitive hemostasis through band ligation requires endoscopy. Hemodynamic stabilization first; transport with monitoring and airway support.

opts.text: Administer scheduled lactulose for ongoing encephalopathy management; orient family to ICU plan
opts.rat: LAST in this acute sequence - encephalopathy management is ongoing rather than time-critical. Family education essential as patient may not be able to participate in decisions.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After EGD with variceal band ligation, octreotide, and 4 units PRBC + 4 FFP, classify each finding at 24 hours.

opts.text: BP 118/72, HR 88, off vasopressors, lactate 1.6 mmol/L
opts.rat: Hemodynamic stabilization with normalized lactate signals successful resuscitation and resolved tissue hypoperfusion - the core goal of the first 24 hours.

opts.text: Hemoglobin stable at 8.4 g/dL on serial draws over 12 hours; no further hematemesis or melena
opts.rat: Stable Hgb without additional bleeding indicates successful endoscopic hemostasis. The first 24-48 hours post-EGD are the highest-risk window for rebleeding.

opts.text: Mental status improved to oriented x2; asterixis decreased; ammonia 76 mcg/dL (down from 118)
opts.rat: Lactulose plus evacuation of the blood load is reducing ammonia and improving encephalopathy. Continued lactulose titration to 2-3 soft stools daily is the goal.

opts.text: Sudden recurrence of bright red hematemesis with HR rising to 130 and BP dropping to 88/54
opts.rat: REBLEEDING - occurs in 30-40% within first 5 days. Re-activate MTP, repeat EGD; if not controllable endoscopically, consider TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) or balloon tamponade as bridge.

opts.text: Fever 38.9°C, abdominal tenderness, ascitic fluid analysis with PMN >250/mm3
opts.rat: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) - even on prophylaxis. Switch to therapeutic antibiotic dosing, give IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) which reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality.

opts.text: Urine output declining from 60 to 18 mL/hr; creatinine rising 1.6 -> 2.4 -> 3.1 mg/dL despite adequate volume resuscitation
opts.rat: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) - dreaded complication of cirrhotic decompensation. Stop nephrotoxins, give albumin + terlipressin/midodrine + octreotide, evaluate for liver transplant. Mortality without transplant is high.

opts.text: Mild bilateral pretibial edema; ascites unchanged
opts.rat: Chronic findings of cirrhosis with portal hypertension - neither acutely improving nor worsening. Manage with sodium restriction and diuretics once acute phase resolves.

opts.text: Patient inquiring about alcohol cessation resources and accepting referral to hepatology and addiction medicine
opts.rat: Engagement with recovery and follow-up is a meaningful psychosocial outcome. Continued sobriety is the single most important intervention for survival in alcoholic cirrhosis.

title: Anaphylaxis from IV Antibiotic
subtitle: 42 y/o female, sudden reaction 10 minutes into ceftriaxone infusion
nursesNote: 1420 - 42 y/o female admitted yesterday for community-acquired pneumonia, started on IV ceftriaxone 1 g q24h (first lifetime dose). Documented allergy on chart: 'sulfa - rash as a child.' No prior beta-lactam exposure. Approximately 10 minutes into the second dose, patient pressed call light reporting itchy throat, lip swelling, and 'feeling of impending doom.' On nurse arrival: diffuse urticaria across chest and arms, periorbital and lip edema, audible inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice, retractions, accompanied by abdominal cramping and one episode of vomiting. Antibiotic infusion immediately stopped. Rapid response activated. Patient anxious, alert, seated upright, gripping the bed rail. IM epinephrine 0.3 mg given to lateral thigh; IV access established; oxygen via non-rebreather. ICU notified.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish anaphylaxis (per NIAID/WAO criteria) in this client.

opts.text: Acute onset (minutes) of skin/mucosal involvement (urticaria, lip and periorbital edema) WITH respiratory compromise (stridor, hoarseness, retractions, SpO2 89%)
opts.rat: NIAID Criterion 1: acute illness with skin/mucosal involvement PLUS respiratory or cardiovascular compromise. Stridor and hoarseness signal upper airway edema - the most lethal feature requiring emergent epinephrine.

opts.text: Hypotension (BP 78/44) developing rapidly after exposure to a likely allergen (first lifetime ceftriaxone dose given previously, now second exposure)
opts.rat: NIAID Criterion 3: reduced BP after known/likely allergen exposure. First exposure sensitizes; SECOND exposure causes the reaction (IgE-mediated). The temporal relationship to drug administration is diagnostic.

opts.text: Two or more organ systems involved (skin + respiratory + GI + cardiovascular) within minutes of trigger exposure
opts.rat: NIAID Criterion 2: rapid onset involving 2+ systems. This patient has all four - the criterion is easily met. Multi-system involvement distinguishes anaphylaxis from isolated urticaria or asthma.

opts.text: Documented sulfa allergy on chart
opts.rat: Sulfa is NOT cross-reactive with cephalosporins (different drug classes). The chart allergy is irrelevant to this reaction - the cue is the current ceftriaxone reaction itself, not the prior sulfa history.

opts.text: WBC 14.8 K/uL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated - consistent with her admission diagnosis of pneumonia, not the anaphylaxis. Background data, not an anaphylaxis cue.

opts.text: Tryptase pending
opts.rat: Tryptase is a CONFIRMATORY lab drawn during the event for diagnostic certainty later - it is NOT used to diagnose anaphylaxis acutely. Treatment must NEVER wait for tryptase results.

opts.text: Patient gripping bed rail and stating 'feeling of impending doom'
opts.rat: This is a real and characteristic patient experience in anaphylaxis (catecholamine surge + hypoxemia), but as a cue it's nonspecific - many critical illnesses present this way. The objective findings are stronger.

opts.text: Hospital admission yesterday for community-acquired pneumonia
opts.rat: Background admission context - explains why she's on the antibiotic but is not a cue OF the anaphylactic reaction.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to the underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: IgE antibodies formed during the first ceftriaxone exposure cross-link on mast cells and basophils during re-exposure, triggering massive release of histamine, tryptase, and leukotrienes
opts.rat: Type I (immediate) hypersensitivity - the textbook mechanism. First exposure sensitizes; second exposure triggers degranulation. Why patients can react to a drug they 'tolerated before' (Lewis Med-Surg 11e).

opts.text: Histamine causes capillary leak (hypotension), bronchoconstriction (wheeze), and laryngeal edema (stridor) - explaining the simultaneous distributive shock and airway compromise
opts.rat: The triple threat of histamine. Capillary leak shifts massive volumes from the intravascular space to the interstitium - hence why fluid resuscitation is needed alongside epinephrine.

opts.text: Epinephrine reverses anaphylaxis through alpha-1 (vasoconstriction reverses hypotension), beta-1 (cardiac output), and beta-2 (bronchodilation, mast cell stabilization) effects - it is the only first-line treatment that addresses ALL components
opts.rat: Why epinephrine is FIRST. Antihistamines, steroids, and bronchodilators are ADJUNCTS - none reverse hypotension or laryngeal edema effectively. Delay in epinephrine administration is the most common cause of fatal anaphylaxis.

opts.text: IV epinephrine 1 mg should be given immediately as first-line in all anaphylaxis cases
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. IM 0.3-0.5 mg in the lateral thigh (vastus lateralis) is FIRST-LINE for stable patients - rapid absorption with safer cardiovascular profile. IV epi is reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory shock and given as a 1:100,000 (10 mcg) dose, not 1:1,000.

opts.text: Diphenhydramine and methylprednisolone are first-line treatments and should precede epinephrine
opts.rat: BACKWARDS. Antihistamines treat itching/hives only - they do NOT treat airway edema or hypotension. Steroids take 4-6 hours to act. Both are adjuncts AFTER epinephrine. This delay error has killed patients.

opts.text: A biphasic reaction can occur 4-12 hours after initial recovery, requiring observation even after symptoms resolve
opts.rat: Wait - this is also CORRECT but the question asks for THREE. Biphasic reactions occur in 5-20% of cases. Mandates 4-6 hour observation minimum (often longer). The other three options are stronger pathophysiology choices for this question.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the differentials from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Anaphylaxis to ceftriaxone (IgE-mediated)
opts.rat: Classic temporal relationship to drug exposure, multi-system involvement, meets NIAID criteria. Diagnosis is clinical and immediate.

opts.text: Anaphylactoid (non-IgE) reaction
opts.rat: Same clinical picture but mechanism doesn't require prior sensitization. Treatment is identical. Distinction is academic in the acute phase.

opts.text: Septic shock from worsening pneumonia
opts.rat: Could explain hypotension and tachycardia but does not explain stridor, urticaria, lip edema, or temporal relationship to drug. Worth ruling out as concurrent process.

opts.text: Acute pulmonary embolism
opts.rat: Hospitalized, immobile patient at risk - but no calf signs, and the skin findings make anaphylaxis far more likely. Different physiology entirely.

opts.text: Vasovagal syncope
opts.rat: Bradycardia (not tachycardia), no skin or airway findings, and resolves with positioning. Does not fit.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions for active anaphylaxis with airway and circulatory compromise.

opts.text: Stop the ceftriaxone infusion and disconnect the IV tubing from the catheter to prevent residual drug delivery
opts.rat: Removing the trigger is foundational. Simply stopping the pump leaves drug in the IV tubing that can still be flushed in - the line itself must be disconnected and replaced with new tubing for any subsequent infusions.

opts.text: Administer IM epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1,000) in the lateral thigh; repeat every 5-15 minutes if symptoms persist
opts.rat: The single most important intervention - reverses all components of anaphylaxis. Up to 35% of patients require a second dose. Lateral thigh (vastus lateralis) provides faster absorption than deltoid.

opts.text: Place patient supine with legs elevated (unless airway compromise dominates), administer high-flow oxygen, and bolus IV crystalloid 1-2 L wide open for the distributive shock component
opts.rat: Distributive shock from massive capillary leak requires aggressive volume resuscitation. Up to 35% of intravascular volume can shift in the first 10 minutes. Trendelenburg/legs-up positioning improves venous return; sitting up is preferred only when airway edema is the dominant problem.

opts.text: Administer IV diphenhydramine 50 mg as the FIRST priority
opts.rat: An adjunct, never first-line. Treats itch/hives but not airway or hypotension. Giving it BEFORE epinephrine delays the only life-saving treatment - a documented cause of anaphylaxis death.

opts.text: Administer IV methylprednisolone 125 mg as the FIRST priority
opts.rat: Steroids take 4-6 hours to take effect and may help PREVENT biphasic reactions, but do nothing in the acute phase. Adjunct, not first-line.

opts.text: Hold epinephrine until BP drops below 70/40 to avoid 'overtreatment'
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. The threshold for IM epinephrine in suspected anaphylaxis is LOW - any combination of skin findings + respiratory or cardiovascular involvement. Waiting for severe hypotension is a documented contributor to anaphylaxis fatality.

opts.text: Elevate the head of bed to 60 degrees regardless of BP to manage stridor
opts.rat: If hypotension dominates, lying with legs elevated improves venous return. Sit up only if airway compromise is the primary concern AND the patient is hemodynamically supportable. Position must match physiology.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: STOP the antibiotic infusion and disconnect the IV tubing from the catheter
opts.rat: FIRST - removing the trigger is immediate and free. Continued exposure undoes any treatment effort. Replace tubing with new fluid-only setup before giving any IV medication.

opts.text: Administer IM epinephrine 0.3 mg to the lateral thigh, call for rapid response
opts.rat: SECOND - the only intervention that reverses airway edema and hypotension. IM is faster and safer than IV in stable patients. Already started in the scenario - readiness for repeat dosing in 5-15 min is the key.

opts.text: Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather, position appropriately for dominant compromise (supine with legs up if hypotension; upright if airway dominant), prepare for possible intubation
opts.rat: THIRD - oxygenation and positioning. Anesthesia/critical care should be at bedside; difficult airway equipment ready as edema can rapidly close the airway.

opts.text: Establish second large-bore IV; bolus 1-2 L 0.9% NS rapidly; prepare to start IV epinephrine drip if refractory
opts.rat: FOURTH - circulatory support for distributive shock component. Refractory anaphylaxis (no response to 2-3 IM epi doses) requires IV epinephrine infusion.

opts.text: Administer adjunct medications (IV diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone, H2 blocker, nebulized albuterol if wheeze persists); document allergy as ANAPHYLAXIS in chart and update wristband
opts.rat: LAST - adjuncts that don't reverse the immediate threat but reduce symptom duration and biphasic reaction risk. Allergy documentation prevents future re-exposure - one of the most important nursing safety actions.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After IM epinephrine, fluid resuscitation, and adjunct therapy, classify each finding over the next 6-24 hours.

opts.text: BP 118/72, HR 86, SpO2 97% on room air, lungs clear, voice normal
opts.rat: Hemodynamic and respiratory recovery within 30-60 minutes of treatment is the expected response to a single dose of IM epinephrine in mild-moderate anaphylaxis.

opts.text: Urticaria fading; lip edema reduced; no new lesions developing
opts.rat: Cutaneous resolution typically lags airway and circulatory recovery by minutes to hours. Continued resolution without new lesions confirms degranulation is settling.

opts.text: Lactate down from 3.6 to 1.2 mmol/L; ABG normalizing
opts.rat: Lactate clearance reflects restored tissue perfusion - the most reliable end-organ marker that resuscitation succeeded.

opts.text: 4 hours after apparent recovery, sudden return of stridor, hypotension, and urticaria with no re-exposure
opts.rat: BIPHASIC REACTION - 5-20% of anaphylaxis cases. Treat as new anaphylaxis: IM epinephrine, fluids, adjuncts. This is exactly why guidelines mandate 4-6+ hour observation post-recovery.

opts.text: New persistent wheeze with worsening dyspnea and oxygen requirement increasing despite resolved skin findings
opts.rat: Protracted anaphylaxis with refractory bronchospasm - escalate to IV epinephrine infusion, continuous albuterol, and consider intubation. May represent ongoing mediator release.
